---
title: "Part III. Computational Bayesian Methods"
---

## Part Overview

Part III covers the numerical machinery that turns formal posterior distributions into usable approximations. The emphasis is on both mathematical justification and practical workflow.

## Part Learning Goals

By the end of Part III, learners should be able to:

- explain what posterior quantity is being approximated and by which numerical mechanism;
- implement basic Monte Carlo and MCMC procedures;
- tune and diagnose Metropolis-Hastings and HMC-type samplers;
- compare MCMC, variational, and sequential methods critically.

## Fully Written Chapters In This Repository

- [Numerical integration and approximation](../chapters/computation/numerical-integration-and-approximation.qmd)
- [Monte Carlo methods](../chapters/computation/monte-carlo-methods.qmd)
- [Markov chain Monte Carlo](../chapters/computation/markov-chain-monte-carlo.qmd)
- [Advanced MCMC: HMC and NUTS](../chapters/computation/advanced-mcmc-hmc.qmd)
- [Variational inference](../chapters/computation/variational-inference.qmd)
- [Sequential and online Bayes](../chapters/computation/sequential-and-online-bayes.qmd)

## Chapter 7. Numerical Integration and Approximation

### Core question

What can be done before MCMC, and when are deterministic or semi-deterministic approximations sufficient?

### Section blueprint

- Quadrature in low dimensions.
- Laplace approximation.
- Importance sampling.
- Bridge sampling.
- Thermodynamic integration overview.
- Variational ideas preview.

### Derivations that must appear

- Laplace approximation from second-order Taylor expansion of the log posterior.
- Importance sampling identity and variance formula.
- Self-normalized importance sampling.
- Bridge sampling intuition through ratio-of-normalizing-constants arguments.

### Computational lab

- Compare exact integration, Laplace approximation, and importance sampling on a one-dimensional posterior.
- Use bridge sampling software or pseudocode to estimate a marginal likelihood.

### Common pitfalls

- Applying Laplace approximation to multi-modal or heavily skewed posteriors.
- Ignoring weight degeneracy in importance sampling.
- Using marginal likelihood approximations as black boxes.

### Exercises

- Derive the Laplace approximation for a scalar posterior around its mode.
- Show how importance weights behave under tail mismatch.

### Core references

- @robert2004
- @mackay2003
- @gelman2013

## Chapter 8. Monte Carlo Methods

### Core question

How does simple sampling approximate posterior expectations, and how should Monte Carlo error be quantified?

### Section blueprint

- Monte Carlo integration.
- Law of large numbers in simulation.
- Monte Carlo standard error.
- Variance reduction: antithetic variables, control variates, stratification.
- Effective sample size ideas in independent sampling.

### Derivations that must appear

- Unbiasedness of Monte Carlo estimators.
- Monte Carlo CLT and variance formula.
- Control variate optimal coefficient.
- ESS interpretation under weighted samples.

### Computational lab

- Estimate posterior means by direct simulation in a conjugate model.
- Compare variance reduction methods empirically.
- Plot Monte Carlo error decay on log-log axes.

### Common pitfalls

- Reporting Monte Carlo estimates without uncertainty.
- Confusing independent-sample ESS with MCMC ESS.
- Using a control variate with poorly estimated correlation.

### Exercises

- Derive the variance of a Monte Carlo estimator.
- Construct an antithetic estimator for a monotone function of a uniform random variable.
- Estimate ESS for an importance-sampling run.

### Core references

- @robert2004
- @casella2002

### Full chapter

- [Monte Carlo methods](../chapters/computation/monte-carlo-methods.qmd)

## Chapter 9. Markov Chain Monte Carlo

### Core question

How can a Markov chain be designed to have the posterior as its invariant distribution?

### Section blueprint

- Markov chain preliminaries.
- Detailed balance and reversibility.
- Metropolis and Metropolis-Hastings algorithms.
- Gibbs sampling.
- Blocked and collapsed Gibbs sampling.
- Adaptive MCMC overview.
- Mixing, autocorrelation, ESS, and convergence.

### Derivations that must appear

- Detailed balance proof for Metropolis-Hastings.
- Acceptance ratio derivation.
- Gibbs sampling as exact conditional replacement.
- Autocorrelation-based ESS formula and interpretation.

### Computational lab

- Random-walk Metropolis in `R`.
- Gibbs sampler for a conjugate normal model.
- Tuning proposal scale and comparing acceptance rates.

### Common pitfalls

- Equating acceptance rate with overall sampler quality.
- Using a single chain and overtrusting visual convergence.
- Ignoring high autocorrelation when reporting Monte Carlo precision.

### Exercises

- Derive the MH ratio for an independence sampler.
- Show that Gibbs sampling satisfies detailed balance.
- Compare random-walk proposals with different scales on the same target.

### Core references

- @metropolis1953
- @hastings1970
- @geman1984
- @andrieu2003
- @brooks2011

### Full sample chapter

- [Markov chain Monte Carlo: derivation, tuning, and diagnostics](../chapters/computation/markov-chain-monte-carlo.qmd)

## Chapter 10. Advanced MCMC

### Core question

How do gradient-informed samplers exploit geometry to explore high-dimensional posteriors more efficiently than random walks?

### Section blueprint

- Hamiltonian Monte Carlo.
- Hamiltonian dynamics and potential energy.
- Leapfrog integration.
- No-U-Turn Sampler.
- Step size and mass matrix adaptation.
- Warmup and adaptation schedules.
- Centered versus non-centered parameterization.
- Slice sampling and elliptical slice sampling.
- Riemannian manifold ideas overview.
- Reversible jump MCMC overview.

### Derivations that must appear

- Hamiltonian equations for position and momentum.
- Leapfrog update map and symplectic intuition.
- Metropolis correction for discretization error.
- Why reparameterization changes geometry but not the posterior.

### Computational lab

- Visualize HMC trajectories on a correlated Gaussian target.
- Compare centered and non-centered eight-schools parameterizations in Stan.
- Diagnose divergences and E-BFMI.

### Common pitfalls

- Increasing `adapt_delta` without addressing geometric pathologies.
- Misinterpreting divergences as merely numerical annoyances.
- Forgetting that tuning improves exploration only when the model geometry is sensible.

### Exercises

- Derive leapfrog updates for a separable Hamiltonian.
- Explain why HMC avoids random-walk behavior in smooth targets.
- Analyze a funnel-shaped posterior and propose a reparameterization.

### Core references

- @neal2011
- @hoffman2014
- @betancourt2017

## Chapter 11. Variational Inference

### Core question

How can posterior inference be reframed as an optimization problem over tractable approximating distributions?

### Section blueprint

- KL divergence and variational families.
- ELBO.
- Mean-field approximation.
- Coordinate ascent VI.
- Stochastic VI.
- ADVI.
- Amortized VI overview.
- Comparison with MCMC.

### Derivations that must appear

- ELBO derivation from $\log p(y)$.
- Mean-field coordinate updates in a conjugate setting.
- Gradient estimators and stochastic optimization overview.
- Forward versus reverse KL geometry discussion.

### Computational lab

- Derive a simple coordinate ascent VI procedure in a conjugate model.
- Compare ADVI-style approximations with MCMC on a skewed posterior.

### Common pitfalls

- Assuming VI error is negligible because optimization converged.
- Underestimating posterior variance due to mean-field assumptions.
- Using ELBO values to compare arbitrary models without care.

### Exercises

- Derive the ELBO identity.
- Show how a mean-field factorization removes posterior dependence.
- Compare reverse-KL behavior with a multi-modal target conceptually.

### Core references

- @blei2017
- @kucukelbir2017

## Chapter 12. Sequential and Online Bayesian Computation

### Core question

How can posterior inference be updated over time when data arrive sequentially or latent states evolve dynamically?

### Section blueprint

- Sequential importance sampling and resampling.
- Particle filters.
- Sequential Monte Carlo.
- Particle MCMC overview.
- Online updating and streaming Bayes.
- State-space models.
- Hidden Markov models.
- Dynamic linear models.

### Derivations that must appear

- Recursive Bayesian filtering identity.
- Particle weight updates and normalization.
- Effective sample size for particle systems.
- Kalman filter equations in the linear Gaussian case.
- Forward algorithm for hidden Markov models.

### Computational lab

- Implement a bootstrap particle filter for a simple state-space model.
- Compare Kalman filtering with simulation in a local-level model.
- Track weight degeneracy over time.

### Common pitfalls

- Ignoring particle degeneracy.
- Using sequential methods without checking numerical stability.
- Confusing filtering, smoothing, and prediction targets.

### Exercises

- Derive the recursive filtering equation.
- Implement multinomial resampling and study its variability.
- Write the forward recursion for a finite-state HMM.

### Core references

- @doucet2001
- @delmoral2004
- @gelman2013
